title: Hannah Muniz -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Hannah Muniz -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi.
Can you hear me okay?
Yes.
How about you?
Can you hear me?
Good.
Yeah, I can hear you perfectly.
Amazing.
How is your Thursday going?
It's good.
Yeah, it's, I'm based in Seattle.
The weather's been good.
It's, well, it's been a bit, you know, hot for me.
But how about you?
How's your week been?
It's good.
I'm on the other side of the country in Rhode Island, so I am.
Oh, wow.
Okay.
I'm waiting for my lunch to be delivered, but I think we'll be good.
And, yeah.
Yeah.
So thank you for scheduling.
Time with us and chatting and being willing to chat.
Have you chatted with Marilia already or anybody?
Yes, Marilia.
Yeah, I chatted with her a few days ago.
Okay, perfect.
Well, I can give you my take on the role.
I'm sure she gave you a little bit of context.

And then give you some background on myself, learn a little bit about you, dive into your experience, and then I'll try and leave like five to ten minutes, probably five minutes at the end just for general questions.
Yeah, that's that comes up.
But also feel free to ask as we're going through things.
So I lead customer operations at GrowthX, which means today, we might change, startup world, means managing kind of like the process of how we generate content and how our teams are put together.
For, and because we're a services organization, so how we put our teams together and the processes that we put in place so that we can scale our services for our clients.

That is, I'm not as much on the technical side, but this role kind of is a balance between somebody that is technical, has worked in AI workflows, and is familiar with kind of really specifically prompting is really important.
And thinking about inputs that create great outputs for content.
And then also somebody who will influence clients, would be necessarily an account lead.

We have a separate role for that, that does a little bit more of like the high level strategy, but more of like a project manager execute on the strategy, make sure that the content that we're generating is good.
A bit of a taste maker for our clients and aligning kind of what we're creating with.
With what we heard about the brand, voice, tone, things like that.
So that's a little bit about the role.
We work in pods, which are essentially every client has right now three people that help support the client.
So there's like that account, they're called account directors today.
And then this technical managing editor is kind of that project manager layer.
And then there are content managers right now who are really day-to-day generating the content, kind of taking a first pass and like doing V1 of the output.
But I think you'd be working really closely with that content manager.
On the other side, you know, we do have like a full eng team.
And so we do have like a client ops team that supports.
But the more that, know, kind of about what would.

What makes a good output, the more that you can inform as we build, this is all built on our own internal platform, all the work that we do, the more that you can help inform and improve that platform, because it's about a month old at this point, so.
Wow, yeah, I knew y'all were a newer company, but I wasn't sure like how new some of this was, so.

Yeah, so we were doing a lot of this work in air ops before, but, you know, we wanted more flexibility and own kind of like the end-to code, a lot of the air ops workflows, like we had like coded prompts in, and so we just wanted to have on the end-to-end experience, give ourselves more like flexibility and set direction for where we wanted to go with the product.
Perfect.
So, that's the background.
you have any questions before we jump in?
I think I'm good right now.
I'll probably have some at the end, but I appreciate the overview of how y'all are set up.
Perfect.
Okay, well, tell me about yourself.
Yeah, well, I'm Hannah.
It's great to meet you again.
I have been working in digital content for about 10 years now.
Started as a writer, then kind of, you know, pivoted to more content manager, editor, and grew in that role to managing editor, my most recent role.
My last job was at a company called Red Ventures.
It's a big digital marketing corporation.
I worked mainly for our higher education vertical.
And so I was overseeing a few different education websites, mostly our main flagship site, and had a team of anywhere from four to six writers and editors under me.
And so we worked a lot on kind of a mix of content.
We focused a lot on more transactional content on the site.
So we had a blog, but you know, it wasn't, it wasn't just a bloggy, like, okay, brand building, you know, kind of blog.
It was, we were a lead generating company.
And so we had to make sure that we were working closely with our SEO team and our customer experience team to ensure that, you know, we were getting people to convert.
And, you know, creating, of course, good content.
So did a lot of that, a lot of experience in SEO and managing, you know, tons of projects at once.
We always were pivoting and we always had to change direction.
Like every few months, we were pretty much, you know, having to go through like a reorg or something like that or a new team.
So I'm very used to like having to pivot quickly and change and sometimes having to like stop a project midway and then, you know, start something new.
So a lot of that at the company I was at.
And yeah, a lot of cross-functional projects.
worked with a lot of different teams, mostly with our SEO team, but other editors.
Like I said, customer experience, engineering as well.
My last year there, I was a co-owner of, I was co-in charge of rolling out a proprietary AI tool that we had.
It was essentially like an AI editorial platform.
So it had capabilities similar to ChatGPT.
We tested it for generative content or, you know, we generated content with it to put on our site.
So So
We were testing it for that, constantly providing feedback, you know, to the engineering team.
We had like an AI liaison that I was, you know, always providing the feedback to.
I would collect the feedback from my team.
So we got really used to prompt engineering, you know, seeing what worked.
Yeah, just providing a lot of feedback on the tool.
And it was a really good experience.
I think the tool was very early stages.
So we were very much the guinea pigs at the beginning and beta testing it.
But I'm really grateful for that, because I think I learned a lot about AI during that.
Anyway, that was a lot.
But if there's anything you want me to elaborate on, happy to do so.
No, that's perfect.
I mean, I'd love to learn a little bit more about the tool, like what you were building.
And how, like, I guess I'm curious, what were the inputs?
And what did you learn over the course of time about how to improve those inputs to make good output?
Yeah, so, I mean, it was definitely a learning experience.
It's I had a...
I had somebody under me, one of my direct reports, who was more versed in, you know, working with AI tools, and so I actually, she gave me a lot of great tips along the way.
So the tool itself, just to be clear, too, it did generate content.
It's had kind of a chat GPT sort of, you know, function, but it also had other things to it.
It was essentially like a huge editorial platform that would replace Google Docs for us, and so it also had things like it would do research for you.
You know, you put in topics, would create an outline or a spec for you.
It would do, give you like a score based on how it meets SEO expectations and things like that.

So it had a lot of different tools and features to it, but yeah, the main one we were working on for a while was that generative context, and so I had no idea how to do any kind of prompt engineering in the beginning, so I was giving it very long, you know, complicated instructions, and it wasn't always understanding what I was asking it to do, and it ended up being, you know, like 10 drafts trying to get it to work, and so the more I worked with it, and the more I talked to that one.
Director Porter I had who had worked with AI before, you know, I kind of realized what types of prompts worked better.
One thing being that you can't overwhelm it, you can't give it like 100 different things to do at once.
And, you know, just focusing on the main things that are most important, at least in the beginning.
And then if you have to, you know, continue to touch up certain areas of it, we did have the ability to, you know, have it regenerate like just one section at a time.
So it was always good to kind of start broad, like, okay, overall, what are the absolute things we should have in this?
Trying to keep it as concise as possible.
And obviously the instructions as clear as we can.
And then from there, I would kind of, you know, nitpick a little bit in terms of the sections.

If I really felt like it wasn't working, you know, would give it a little more in-depth instruction, a little more detailed or nuanced, you know, like maybe about word count or just separating it out.
And of course, there was always a bit of human intervention at the end, too.
Like, there were just some things our tool wasn't doing well.
And so...
so...
so...
so...
Thank

You know, grabbing authoritative sources, it did struggle with, so that would be, that was something that probably just was a limitation of our tool, but we were used to, you know, having to, like, fact check a bit, too, and, you know, check for hallucinations, and so, yeah, I mean, I'm obviously still learning, like, I really enjoyed using it, I'm sure there's a lot more prompt engineering that I still need to know, but, yeah, I mean, it's been fun, I've been doing, of course, I've been working with ChatGPT, I just had my own time, too, and trying to get better with that, and that's been, that's been just, like, like, yeah, it's own experience, so.
Oh, sure, yeah.

It's interesting, our tool does a lot of those, like, we have a lot of workflows that are built into the platform, and you kind of, for each client, we're plucking which workflow we want, and it starts as a template, but the template is kind of bad, and not, doesn't have enough specific instruction.
Yeah, and then there's maybe five or six steps that go through, and you kind of have to pull different.
And we call them artifacts of like for instruction at each stage.
But yeah, we have like a content scoring, which is still in development, but then we have like, like article ideation and outline stages.
Oh, cool.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, it sounds very similar.
That's cool.
Wow.
So it sounds like you led the work on, you mentioned that this was like the homepage for Red Ventures.
Talk to me, have you ever like worked for like client facing at all?
Not a ton, to be honest, but I have.
So most of my history has been in like B2C, not B2B.
But it's definitely an area I've been wanting to explore more.
I did have a little bit of experience, again, at Red Ventures, working with a couple of school partners for sponsored content.
So we did, this was more in 2023 or 2022, I can't remember.
We.
We had a kind of an initiative to try to, you know, start doing sponsored content at the time, we ended up kind of divesting from that just for other reasons.
But at the time, I was put in charge of that.
And it was me and one writer, one internal writer.
And so we worked together to, we were given sort of the, the overview of what the client was looking for, what they were hoping to do.
We brainstormed ideas or looked for existing content that we thought that they could sponsor.
And yeah, brainstormed ideas for new content.
And so we would put together slides and we did do, you know, like a pitch deck for the client and communicated a lot with them throughout the process as well.
Like once we were drafting, you know, we would make sure that we were on the right line, know, or along the same lines, you know, on the right path.
So we would share ideas and outlines and rough drafts with them, first drafts with them in the beginning.
Yeah, so I was very comfortable doing that.
Like I had a lot of fun, actually.
I wish we could have continued that more.
But, you know, limitations of the company at the time, it just wasn't the direction we were going to go.
But it was really fun.
And I really enjoyed.
I like putting together slide decks, and, you know, it was really fun just pitching ideas to people, too, so.
Yeah, totally.
I mean, it sounds like that's very similar to what this role would be doing.
It's really, like, the updates, the sharing, like, and refinement piece.
That refinement aspect is really a partnership and conversation.
But I'm sure that the experience that you have from Red Ventures would also bring that, just, like, in terms of SEO and performance.
Um, so you managed a team before.
Can you talk to me a little bit about how you, um, think about prioritizing and when, when, um, you've got, like, competing projects?
Um, how do you organize your time and also organize, like, the team that you're working with?
Yeah, I mean, we constantly had different priorities.
Like, they were always shifting.
Um, but yeah, lots of projects at once often.
Um, first things first, you know, I like to get, I would, if I could, I would try to get clarity from my manager or whoever, you know, knew more about the projects just to
And sure that I was prioritizing correctly as a manager.
Obviously, that's not always, you're not always able to do that.
You have to be comfortable working in ambiguity.
And so I did have to make decisions myself, too, for my team.
You know, our main goals were, you know, producing or updating revenue-driven content.
So that was always kind of our fallback of, like, if we have to prioritize something, we're going to prioritize what makes us money.
There were a lot of ideas we had, you know, and a lot of side projects that were more focused on brand building and just driving, you know, page views and sessions to the site.
But often those would have to be, you know, they were going to be a lesser priority than the ones that were going to make us money or anything that was a little more seasonal.
Seasonality is obviously a big thing in higher education.
You have, like, you know, the summer season, you have the application season.
So making sure that we were hitting these deadlines, too, that were really important, seasonally speaking.
So those would always take priority as well.
And just, yeah, if there was any project where we did a lot of SEO tests with our
SEO team.
So, you know, in order to do these tests and to monitor them and see if the performance, see if they're working, you have to get it live.
And so a lot of that, too, was just working with them to make sure, like, okay, when do we really want this test live?
How long are we going to run the test?
If it's going to be, you know, a couple of months, we need to get it live sooner so that we can actually start tracking the performance of it.
But so it was just a lot of back and forth communicating.
And, you know, for me, it's like, like I said, it's kind of just going with my instinct on, you know, what I think makes sense to prioritize.
And so I always tried to clearly communicate that with my own direct reports.
And, you know, I always make myself available to like, if anyone does have questions, or they're really stuck, and they have no idea what to do.
I mean, that's what I'm there for.
So that's, you know, I got really comfortable doing that a lot with my, with my team.
And yeah, I mean, it's, it's something that I'm just very used to is juggling a lot.
So I have no idea with it.
I feel like I'm pretty organized.
So it's, yeah, it's something that I'm comfortable doing.
Yeah.
Um, I'm just
Taking notes, sorry.
Oh, no, you're fine.
Yeah.
I know the AI tool, too, is taking notes.
I saw it joined before you did, and I was like, oh, cool.
We have those, but, like, also, yesterday, I was looking, I was trying to find a meeting from July 7th, because, like, I take notes, too.
I just, like, it's the way I am of age when there were notes.
And so I can't remember things unless it's being written somewhere, and I, like, I'm thinking about what the words.
Editorial, right?
And so I, like, found the notes from July 7th, and I was like, okay, I want to see, like, what we said in that call.
And it couldn't, it was, like, really, really hard to go back that far.
Just, like, loading, because also one of our problems is we have, like, 17 people in a call, and everybody has their own note-taker.
And we, like.
Oh, optimize the note-taker.

So then you have like, if it's an all-hands or like a stand-up meeting with 10 people, you have 10 of the same meeting that's taking up a block and then like scrolling because it's got so much like content.
Oh my gosh, yeah, I didn't think about that.
So it's, yeah, I mean like, and then now, because they want to do AI, the note-taker is like, search with AI.
And I'm like, July 7th, this is the title of the call.
And it's like, we don't have anything for that.
I'm like, need this.
Oh, that's, you know, that's, sometimes AI can be, you know, you have to be a little, to kind of.
Like, can I just filter by date?
No, it has to, AI has to filter by date for me.
I just had that with something else recently.
I can't remember what it was, but I was trying to filter something and it was just not picking up like the simplest keyword.
And I was like, I don't think it was AI, but it was just something else.
Like, did we eliminate metadata in favor of AI?
That sounds very inefficient.
I know, all sorts of levels, and we work for an AI company, but no, not on this, not for this thing, please.
Anyway, sorry, tangent.
Okay, wait, I did have a question.
Subscription driven content, SEO testing.
Oh, I did want to clarify.
Okay, so this is not like a management role.

So the organization is like a little bit weird where that layer of writers is, they work in the pod, but I think they report directly to like our head of content because we're thinking as the tool gets better, and we'll see if this ends up being true, but ideally if the tool gets better, we would move to more of a pool model where they could pick up content and run the workflows as they go.
It remains to be seen how much context.
on the industry or the client is really necessary.
But I think we're going to experiment with that a little bit, which yeah, no, that makes sense.
Yeah, Leah had said that it wasn't a management role.
And I'm 100% fine with that.
I, I like managing and I love like mentoring and helping other people.
But I, it doesn't have to be a part of my job.
It can be kind of more of an unofficial part too.
I'm always happy.
So I'm, yeah, completely fine with that.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.

I mean, I think it ends up being like, it's a lot of coordination and working and like, you know, you do still have to deal with people's priorities and they're feeling like how they're feeling about their day and taking those into account.
Yeah.
forward, but just from like a formal management perspective.

And then I wanted to dive a little bit into, you mentioned like being uncomfortable in situations or, or comfortable in situations where there's not a lot of clarity or things are moving quickly, but can you talk to me a little bit about it?
Time when you had to help make a decision or worked in like a startup-like environment where there wasn't a lot of direction and you moved something forward?
Yeah, I mean, even though, you know, the company I was at was not a startup by any means, it sometimes felt like it just because we were trying so many new things.
And, you know, with the economy, too, we just, have to pivot quickly.
So, yeah, at the beginning, I was there five years, like I said.
And at the very beginning, I admit, like, I was not good with ambiguity.
Like, I didn't like sudden change, you know, and I was like, oh, man, like, this is so hard.
I have to stop doing this and now I have to do this.
But you quickly get used to it when it's just part of your job and it's like every few months.
So it was good for me.
Yeah.
So I, you know, business strategy, it's hard because it's always, it's, I don't want say like, I guess it's like a living and breathing thing in some ways.
know that's kind of, you know, silly to say that.
But, but it's always going to be changing depending on what's working and what's not and just like other factors, like obviously.
Seriously.
AI has grown so much in the past couple of years and what people want for, you know, content on their websites and how that content is found, whether it's through SEO or now, you know, chat, GPT.
So I think part of it is just kind of recognizing, you know, that things are going to change and that's just part of it.
So I had to get comfortable with it.
But in terms of a particular time, I'm trying to think of a very specific time because I feel like it kind of happened a lot just generally, you know, throughout my job there.
But I mean, there were many times where my manager was a, you know, really great person, but they didn't have a lot of clarity often.

And so, you know, there were times recently, even with like the AI tool that we had that we kind of had paused using it for a bit because we were doing this other project that just, you know, we needed like all hands on deck.
And so, you know, I had a few months where I'm like, okay, what are we doing with this tool?
I'm like, what, what are we doing with this AI tool?
Are we going to keep using it?
Are we going to stop using it?
Are we going
And I to get some clarity, and I just wasn't getting any from my manager, because he didn't know.
And like, you know, even the senior leaders just weren't sure what to do with it.
And so I kind of just had to make a decision for myself, you know, and so I talked to my team.
And, you know, we all kind of recognized that we don't think the tool is going to go away.
And obviously, AI, like I said, has changed a lot.
It's not, it's not going away, it's here to stay.
And so we need to get out a way to implement it and use it.
So we just kind of came up with our own plan on how we should use it.

And so we had our own kind of like small goals, like, okay, you know, if this piece feels like, you know, it's easier to research with, you know, this tool or whatever, put it through that, I think we had a minimum to have like, let's aim for, you know, like 1015 pieces a month, or just something that was easy at the time to do.
But that would still feel like we were continuing to test and not lose our own skills with the tool to.
So yeah, that was kind of a time where we really didn't know what we were doing with it.
But we didn't just want to let it, you know, sit there and like collect dust.
So obviously.
We're trying to find ways to keep using it.
Yeah.
Totally.
Um, the, yeah, I think that the piece about, you still want to be a tastemaker, right?
Like, I think that's a phrase Marcel uses a lot, but like, you can't not know if what it's putting out is good and you have to know how to make it good.
Like you have to know how to do the legwork.
Like you have to keep that muscle strong.
Totally.
Yeah.
No, a hundred percent.
And when we had like a month or two without using it, we could tell that we had kind of gotten, you know, we weren't as like, we weren't as good anymore.
So that's why we were also like, okay, we need to get back into this.
Uh, even if no one's telling us directly to do it, we were like, we just got to do it.
So yeah.
Totally.
Um, okay.
Well, we have seven minutes left.
So what questions you have for me?
Yeah.
Um, so I, I stalk everybody on LinkedIn.
Um, you've been at the company, what, like four or five months.
How, could you just give me a sense of like, how are you feeling about the startup and the energy and the cold?
How has it been for you since you're still fairly new?
Yeah, there's a lot of change.
And I think I'm really excited about where we are right now because when I joined, we had some onboarding, but we didn't have the platform yet.
And people were engaging with our AI tooling and AI in general in different levels.
And so, like, efficiency was all over the board and we had sort of an idea of how much content we thought somebody could produce with AI, but not everybody was using AI.
And so, like, metrics and performance and, like, training in AI tools generally and kind of, like, prompting was all very, it was all knotted together and not very organized.
And I think we've come a long way just in the last three months of figuring out, like, well, first we have this platform now.
think platform now.
And so,
And because it's now live, we have more engineering resources to help sort out, like, how to make workflows better.
I think we're starting to ask the right questions about what's not working in the AI workflows, rather than being like, you should use them, they work.
And so, like, from a top-down level, we're starting to be more curious and, like, willing to dive in and troubleshoot.
I think we're starting to get some stability on the client side.
There was a lot of, like, people weren't good fits for startups, and there was too much change in there, and, like, AI is hard.
You know, like, those expectations and not having that training made things really hard.
So, I think there has been probably the first month I was here was, like, turbulent in terms of, like, those changes all happening.
And then in month two, we launched the new tool.
And we, like, we did a, like, content pause so everybody could learn.
So I think we're really stabling out.
We've also, like, come up with a new model of implementation so that customers go through an eight-week, like, calibration period with our, like, sort of top team.
Oh, that's a great idea.
Yeah, so they can be more stable when the rest of our team kind of gets handed off.
It just creates, I think, a little bit less, like, it makes everything more even in terms of, like, client expectations and what a team, like, starts with.
And just, like, performance between pods.
So I think it was a crazy first month.
And I'm really excited about where we're going now.
Oh, there's just so much buzz around, like, what we're doing.
And I think, like, service.
Rises around AI, it's so hard to do, like, people don't realize how much work you need and how much human intervention needs to happen.
I know, yeah.
Make good AI output, that when they try to do it, suddenly they're like, oh, oh yeah, we should probably pay somebody to do this.
And so I think, like, the opportunity is really, really big.
Like, that's awesome to hear that.
Yeah, I think that makes complete sense, too, with all the turbulence.
Like, I kind of expected that just with any startup, too.
And it's like, hey, we're a year old.
Yeah, no, I think that's what the recruiter had told me, too.
I was like, oh, okay, you guys are really new.
Yeah, yeah.
No, and I actually really love that environment.
I, you know, it was, it was nice working at, like, a large corporation.
It was very established, and I had a lot of that.
But, you know, there are downsides to it, too.
Like, I felt at the end, like, you know, my voice wasn't heard as much, and, like, I didn't have any chance to really, like, be innovative or make change or, you know, really affect it.
And so I like the idea of really pivoting and working at a startup.
And, you know, I mean, it's fun being part of something at the beginning, too.
I've always really liked that and figuring out the processes, which there always is something to figure out.
So, yeah, yeah.
Another question I had, I think, well, it sounds like y'all are changing a lot still.
mean, you know, positions, levels might change, too.
But do you think, I guess, based on your limited time there so far, that there would be, like, opportunity to grow at the company?
Can you see that in the future, like, opportunities for, I don't know, like, you know, moving up the chain if leadership was something that I wanted to get into at some point?
Yeah.
I mean, I think the makeup of the pods and the definition of the roles were actually, like, actively in conversation of what every role, like, really should look like.
So even this, you will be joining as...
It's a managing editor, and we have, like, 15 managing editors, but I think, like, we're, we've created, like, a much more solid rubric of what we want and how that person would build.
So you're kind of in, like, a third, probably the third class of hiring for this, which be much closer to, like, the first class.

And again, this probably led to some of the, like, wildness, was, like, director-level people were account managers, and then, like, sort of mid-level content writers, and there was no, like, bridging of that gap.
And so I think both, and it became, it, it, it seemed really hard to imagine, like, that shift.

I think we're hiring for that, that account, account strategist is the next person that we're going to hire that's essentially going to take that director-level role, and that person is going to be, like, it's a little bit of a mid-level.
And you.
You were probably, like, leveled up.
So, yes, like, the goal is to make it so that those, that progression is a little bit more natural.

And, I mean, if we keep signing, like, clients at the rate that we are, then that has to be, like, the promotion track of bringing people up that are familiar with the platform and know how to run these clients.
Yeah.
Great.
Thank you.
I appreciate the transparency about that.
Yeah.
I know we're at time.
Could I ask one?
Yeah.
Small question.
Of course, I was going to ask it, and then I, completely slips my mind.
Give me one sec.
It's to be about, you know what?
I can't remember it.
I guess it wasn't as important as I thought.
Am I allowed to email if I have any questions after?
for sure.
I'll write with you.
Andy at growthx.
Okay.
Thank you so much.
You know, I really appreciated this.
This was great to learn more, and it was great to meet you, too.
Yeah.
Thanks for your time, Hannah.
Have a good one.
Thank you.
Appreciate it.
Bye.
